title: Lesson 4 - Deploying and Sharing Skills
description: Learn the exact directories each platform watches, how precedence works when skills share a name, and how to promote skills from personal sandboxes to shared repositories safely.
learningObjectives: Know the canonical paths for personal and project skills across major AI tools.
Promote a personal skill into a project repository with git-based review.
Explain priority resolution when duplicate skill names exist in multiple locations.
Evaluate community skills for security and quality before installing.

videos.title: Personal Skills: Your Private Toolkit
videos.description: Where to store skills that are just for you — your personal AI configuration that follows you across every project.
scriptOutline: Personal skills live in your home directory — available in every project on your machine.
When to keep skills personal: style preferences, personal checklists, experimental workflows.
Live demo: install the deploy-checklist as a personal skill, open two different projects, show it works in both.
Tip: avoid hardcoding machine-specific paths — use relative references.
talkingPoints: Personal skills are your sandbox — the fastest iteration loop with zero risk to others.
Great personal skills: your preferred commit format, your documentation voice, your review checklist.
Start here. Always. Move to project-level only after the skill proves its value.

videos.title: Project Skills: Sharing with Your Team via Git
videos.description: Promote a skill from personal to project-level so everyone on the team benefits. Enforce review through pull requests.
scriptOutline: Project skills live in the repository — shared via git with your entire team.
When to promote: the skill works consistently AND other people would benefit.
Live demo: move deploy-checklist from ~/.claude/skills/ to .claude/skills/ in the repo.
Open a pull request — show how the team reviews and improves skills like code.
Priority: project skills override personal skills with the same name.
Running example: deploy-checklist is now a project skill, tracked in git.
talkingPoints: Project skills encode team knowledge into the repository — they're living documentation.
Git gives you review (PRs), history (blame), and rollback (revert) for free.
The promotion signal: when you catch yourself telling a teammate 'use my personal skill for this.'

videos.title: Community Skills: Finding and Evaluating Shared Skills
videos.description: How to discover community-built skills, evaluate them for safety, and install them responsibly.
scriptOutline: Evaluation checklist: read SKILL.md, check description, audit scripts/ for destructive commands.
Red flags: rm -rf, curl to unknown URLs, commands that modify files outside the skill directory.
Installation: copy the folder to your personal skills directory first, test, then promote if valuable.
Contributing back: how to share your skills with the community.
talkingPoints: You don't have to write every skill from scratch. The community has already built useful ones.
Treat community skills like any software: read the code before running it.
Fork and adapt rather than editing community skills in place — respect upstream.

textSections.title: Directory Cheat Sheet
textSections.content:
**Personal skills (just for you — available in all projects):**

| Tool | Path |
|------|------|
| Claude Code | `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` |
| GitHub Copilot | `~/.copilot/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` |
| Cursor | `~/.cursor/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` |
| Universal | `~/.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` |

**Project skills (shared via git — available to the team):**

| Tool | Path |
|------|------|
| Claude Code | `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` |
| GitHub Copilot | `.github/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` |
| Cursor | `.cursor/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` |
| Universal | `.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` |

**Note:** GitHub Copilot checks all three project directories. When in doubt, `.agents/skills/` is the most universal.

textSections.title: Priority Resolution: What Wins When Names Clash
textSections.content:
When multiple skills share the same name, the most specific one wins:

1. **Project skills** override **personal skills** (team standards beat personal preferences).
2. **Enterprise skills** override both (organization policies win).

**Example:** You have a personal `code-review` skill. Your project also has a `code-review` skill. The project version is used — because it reflects team conventions, not just yours.

**Tip:** To avoid confusion, use distinct names: `my-code-review` (personal) vs. `code-review` (project).

textSections.title: Promotion Workflow: Personal → Project
textSections.content:
Step-by-step guide to promoting a skill:

**1. Validate the skill works consistently** (you've tested it 3+ times with different inputs).

**2. Copy to the project directory:**
```bash
cp -r ~/.claude/skills/deploy-checklist .claude/skills/deploy-checklist
```

**3. Commit and push:**
```bash
git add .claude/skills/deploy-checklist/
git commit -m "Add deploy-checklist skill for team use"
git push
```

**4. Open a pull request** with:
- What problem the skill solves
- Test evidence (screenshots or test log from Lesson 3)
- How to invoke the skill

**5. (Optional) Remove the personal copy** to avoid shadowing:
```bash
rm -r ~/.claude/skills/deploy-checklist
```

Now everyone on your team gets the skill on their next `git pull`.

textSections.title: Security Checklist for Community Skills
textSections.content:
Before installing any community skill, check:

- [ ] **Read SKILL.md** — does the description match your use case?
- [ ] **Audit scripts/** — look for `rm`, `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, or commands that modify files
- [ ] **Check references/** — no secrets, credentials, or proprietary data
- [ ] **Verify the source** — active repo? Recent commits? Issues addressed?
- [ ] **Install to personal first** — test in your sandbox before promoting to project
- [ ] **Note the commit hash** — document exactly which version you installed

A skill with scripts/ can run commands on your machine. Treat it with the same caution as installing any software.

commonMistakes.mistake: Putting personal preferences in project skills
commonMistakes.explanation: Project skills should reflect team conventions, not individual taste. Keep your personal quirks (favorite formatting, custom abbreviations) in personal skills.

commonMistakes.mistake: Installing community skills without reading them
commonMistakes.explanation: Always read SKILL.md and inspect scripts/ before installing. A skill with 'rm -rf' in a script could delete your files.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to commit and push skill changes
commonMistakes.explanation: Project skills only reach your team through git. Edit → commit → push. If you skip push, nobody else gets the update.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using the wrong directory path for your tool
commonMistakes.explanation: Each tool has specific paths. .claude/skills/ works for Claude Code but may not be discovered by Gemini CLI. Check the cheat sheet.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not removing the personal copy after promotion
commonMistakes.explanation: If both personal and project versions exist, the project version wins. But having two copies creates confusion about which to edit. Clean up after promoting.

reflectionQuestions: Is your skill more of a personal preference or a team convention? Which tier does it belong at?
If your team had a shared skills library, which 3 skills would save the most collective time?
Have you ever wished a colleague followed the same process you do? That's a project skill waiting to be written.
What would your security checklist look like for evaluating skills in a regulated industry?

exercises.title: Path Audit
exercises.description: Verify that the personal and project skill directories exist on your machine. Create any missing directories.
exercises.output: Verified directory paths plus commands used to create any missing ones.
successCriteria: Confirmed the personal skill directory exists for your primary AI tool.
Confirmed the project skill directory exists (or created it).
Noted the exact paths for your OS and tool combination.

exercises.title: Promote Your Skill to Project Level
exercises.description: Move your skill from personal to project directory, commit it to git, and verify it still works from the project location.
exercises.output: A git commit containing your skill in the project skills directory.
successCriteria: Skill copied to the correct project directory.
Committed to git with a descriptive message.
AI tool recognizes the skill from the project directory.
Tested the skill still works after relocation.

exercises.title: Community Skill Evaluation
exercises.description: Find a community skill, run the security checklist, and write a one-paragraph evaluation: adopt, fork, or reject.
exercises.output: Completed security checklist plus your decision with justification.
successCriteria: Found a community skill and noted the source URL + commit hash.
Completed all items on the security checklist.
Wrote a clear decision (adopt/fork/reject) with reasoning.
If adopted, installed to personal directory and tested.

instructorNotes: This lesson bridges 'I can write a skill' and 'my team uses skills.' The promotion workflow is the key moment.
For learners not using git: walk through git add/commit/push basics. This is also a great moment to show how AI tools help with git.
Spend real time on the security checklist. Show what a malicious script looks like vs. a safe one. This builds trust and judgment.
For solo learners: the promotion exercise still works — create a test repo and commit there.
Keep a demo repository ready so learners can watch the full promotion workflow end-to-end.
Community skill repos change frequently. Verify the URLs in resources before the session.
